page: Hetzner Cloud cpx11 is an entry-level virtual server powered by the QEMU hypervisor. It features an x86_64 AMD EPYC CPU running at 2.0 GHz with 2 shared vCPUs and 2 physical cores. The hardware configuration includes 2.0 GB of system memory, providing approximately 0.98 GB per core, alongside a 40 GB local SSD. No dedicated GPUs or complimentary public IPv4 addresses are included in this configuration.

Performance benchmarks indicate that the shared CPU allocation and modest memory capacity place this server in the lower tiers for compute-heavy tasks. The stress-ng single-core and multi-core CPU performance tests rank in the bottom 10 percent, while the PassMark CPU score falls in the bottom 25 percent. Memory bandwidth for both small and large blocks is categorized as weak, and the PassMark memory score is in the bottom 10 percent. However, the system achieves average performance in single-threaded gzip compression and bzip3 decompression, alongside average text generation speeds for small 135M parameter language models.

Given its shared resource model and lower performance profile, the cpx11 offers a highly cost-efficient option for lightweight workloads where resource density and budget are prioritized over raw compute power. It is best suited for basic web serving, development environments, lightweight background tasks, and small-scale testing rather than intensive database operations or high-throughput production applications.
description: Hetzner Cloud cpx11 is an entry-level virtual server featuring 2 shared vCPUs on an AMD EPYC x86_64 processor running at 2.0 GHz, paired with 2.0 GB of RAM and a 40 GB local SSD. Operating under the QEMU hypervisor, this server does not include a GPU or complimentary public IPv4 addresses. Benchmark results place its CPU and memory performance primarily in the weak to poor categories, though it achieves average results in single-threaded gzip compression and small-model text generation. Due to its shared CPU allocation and modest memory footprint, the cpx11 is a cost-effective option designed for lightweight workloads, development environments, and basic utility tasks rather than resource-intensive production applications.
